In [1]:
# Import all necessary Python packages for data analysis and visualization
import pandas as pd                 # For handling dataframes
import numpy as np                  # For numerical operations
import seaborn as sns               # For statistical visualizations
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt     # For plotting graphs
import statsmodels.api as sm        # For regression modeling and statistical tests
from statsmodels.formula.api import ols  # For constructing OLS regression models
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split  # For splitting data into training and testing sets
In [2]:
# Load the data for marketing#
data = pd.read_csv('marketing_sales_data_n.csv')

mean_sales_tv = data.groupby('TV')['Sales'].mean()

# Bar plot for TV
sns.barplot(x='TV', y='Sales', data=data)
plt.title("Mean Sales by TV Category")

# Bar plot for Influencer
sns.barplot(x='Influencer', y='Sales', data=data)
plt.title("Mean Sales by Influencer Size")
plt.show()
In [13]:
# 1. Drop rows with missing values (since very few)- always drop or treat missing before doing more analysis or building model ##
data = data.dropna()
In [14]:
# Drop missing values and reset index
data.reset_index(inplace=True, drop=True)

# Replace spaces in column names with underscores
data.columns = data.columns.str.replace(' ', '_')

# Check the new column names
print(data.columns)

In [22]:
# Create dummy variables for TV and Influencer
data_encoded = pd.get_dummies(data, columns=['TV'], drop_first=True)

# Check the new columns
print(data_encoded.columns)

Index(['Radio', 'Social_Media', 'Influencer', 'Sales', 'TV_Low', 'TV_Medium'], dtype='object')
In [23]:
from statsmodels.formula.api import ols

# Define the formula
formula = 'Sales ~ Radio + TV_Low + TV_Medium'
In [24]:
# Create an OLS model
model = ols(formula, data=data_encoded)
In [25]:
# Fit the model
fitted_model = model.fit()
In [26]:
# Save the results summary to a text file
with open('regression_summary.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write(fitted_model.summary().as_text())
In [27]:
# Display the model results summary
print(fitted_model.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                  Sales   R-squared:                       0.904
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.904
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     1783.
Date:                Sun, 15 Mar 2026   Prob (F-statistic):          1.63e-288
Time:                        08:24:34   Log-Likelihood:                -2714.0
No. Observations:                 572   AIC:                             5436.
Df Residuals:                     568   BIC:                             5453.
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
=====================================================================================
                        coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept           218.5261      6.261     34.902      0.000     206.228     230.824
TV_Low[T.True]     -154.2971      4.929    -31.303      0.000    -163.979    -144.616
TV_Medium[T.True]   -75.3120      3.624    -20.780      0.000     -82.431     -68.193
Radio                 2.9669      0.212     14.015      0.000       2.551       3.383
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       61.244   Durbin-Watson:                   1.870
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):               18.077
Skew:                           0.046   Prob(JB):                     0.000119
Kurtosis:                       2.134   Cond. No.                         142.
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

In [30]:
##normalily calculation now Plot 1: Histogram of the residuals - Plot 2: Q-Q plot of the residuals##
# Calculate the residuals
residuals = fitted_model.resid
In [31]:
# Create a histogram of the residuals
sns.histplot(residuals, bins=30)
plt.title('Histogram of Residuals')
plt.show()
In [32]:
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Create a Q-Q plot of the residuals
sm.qqplot(residuals, line='s')
plt.show()
In [33]:
# Create a scatterplot with fitted values and residuals
sns.scatterplot(x=fitted_model.fittedvalues, y=residuals)

# Add a horizontal line at y = 0
plt.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='--')

plt.xlabel("Fitted Values")
plt.ylabel("Residuals")
plt.title("Residuals vs Fitted Values")
plt.show()
In [34]:
# Create a pairplot of the numeric data to check for multicollinearity
sns.pairplot(data_encoded)
plt.show()

In [43]:
# Import variance_inflation_factor from statsmodels.
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

# Create a subset of the data with the continous independent variables. 
X = data[['Radio','Social_Media']]

# Calculate the variance inflation factor for each variable.
vif = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]

# Create a DataFrame with the VIF results for the column names in X.
df_vif = pd.DataFrame(vif, index=X.columns, columns = ['VIF'])

# Display the VIF results.
df_vif
Out[43]:
VIF
Radio 5.170922
Social_Media 5.170922
In [36]:
# Convert boolean dummy variables to integers
bool_cols = ['TV_Low', 'TV_Medium']
data_encoded[bool_cols] = data_encoded[bool_cols].astype(int)

# Check dtypes to confirm
print(data_encoded.dtypes)

Radio           float64
Social_Media    float64
Influencer       object
Sales           float64
TV_Low            int64
TV_Medium         int64
dtype: object
In [44]:
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

X = data_encoded[['Radio', 'Social_Media']]



# Calculate VIF for each variable
vif = pd.DataFrame()
vif['Variable'] = X.columns
vif['VIF'] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]
print(vif)

       Variable       VIF
0         Radio  5.170922
1  Social_Media  5.170922
In [45]:
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Add constant (intercept term)
# NOTE: This represents the intercept in regression models.
# Many tutorials omit this step when calculating VIF for simplicity,
# which can lead to slightly different VIF values.
# We keep it here for completeness because most OLS models include an intercept.

# Select only continuous numeric predictors
X = data_encoded[['Radio', 'Social_Media']]

# Add constant for VIF calculation
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Calculate VIF
vif = pd.DataFrame()
vif['Variable'] = X.columns
vif['VIF'] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]

# Display the VIF results
print(vif)

       Variable       VIF
0         const  4.713305
1         Radio  1.657894
2  Social_Media  1.657894
